title: PriorityQueue (Java's Built-In Heap)
description: Going deeper than the Queue/Deque preview -- PriorityQueue's min-heap default, custom ordering with Comparator, and why peek/poll are O(1)/O(log n).
objectives: Understand PriorityQueue as Java's built-in heap, defaulting to a min-heap
Use a custom Comparator to build a max-heap
Know peek/poll/offer's time complexities and why they differ

sections.text: In Queue and Deque, we saw PriorityQueue always returns the smallest element first, regardless of insertion order, and promised a full lesson once Heap arrived. Today we go deeper into HOW and WHY.

A tree-based data structure where each parent is ordered relative to its children (smaller for a min-heap, larger for a max-heap), keeping the smallest (or largest) element always accessible at the root.

sections.title: PriorityQueue's core operations
rows: Returns the smallest element WITHOUT removing it
Removes AND returns the smallest element

sections.text: peek() is O(1) because the smallest element is ALWAYS at the root, instantly accessible. poll() is O(log n) because after removing the root, the heap must reorganize itself to restore its ordering property -- this reorganizing is exactly what Manual Heap Implementation (next lesson) shows in full detail.
3. Building a Max-Heap with a Comparator
PriorityQueue only gives a min-heap by default. For a max-heap (largest first), supply a Comparator -- recall Comparable vs Comparator -- that reverses the natural order.

Collections.reverseOrder() (from the Collections Utility Class lesson) provides a Comparator that reverses natural ordering -- passed into the PriorityQueue constructor, it flips the heap's behavior from smallest-first to largest-first.
4. A Real Use Case: Kth Largest Element

sections.title: Why this finds the 2nd largest
points: The heap is only ever allowed to hold k=2 elements -- whenever it grows past that, the SMALLEST is discarded
After processing all of {3,2,1,5,6,4}, only the 2 LARGEST values survive in the heap: {5,6}
The min-heap's root (peek()) is the smaller of those two survivors -- exactly the 2nd largest overall: 5

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Assuming PriorityQueue's toString() or iteration order reflects sorted order -- only poll() repeatedly gives elements in true heap order; the internal array is NOT fully sorted.
Forgetting PriorityQueue defaults to a min-heap -- needing a max-heap requires an explicit Comparator like Collections.reverseOrder().
Confusing peek()'s O(1) with poll()'s O(log n) -- removing requires reorganizing the heap, looking does not.
Using PriorityQueue when you actually need strict FIFO order -- it always prioritizes by value, ignoring insertion order entirely, unlike a plain Queue.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: Does PriorityQueue give elements in sorted order if you iterate over it directly (not via poll())?
questions.answer: No. Only repeatedly calling poll() guarantees elements come out in heap order (smallest/largest first). The internal array backing the heap is not fully sorted, so direct iteration order is unspecified.

questions.question: How do you make a PriorityQueue behave as a max-heap instead of the default min-heap?
questions.answer: Supply a Comparator that reverses the natural ordering, such as new PriorityQueue<>(Collections.reverseOrder()).

questions.question: Why is peek() O(1) but poll() O(log n) on a PriorityQueue?
questions.answer: peek() simply reads the root, which always holds the smallest (or largest, for a max-heap) element -- no work needed. poll() removes the root and then must reorganize the heap to restore its ordering property, which takes O(log n).

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: PriorityQueue is Java's built-in heap, defaulting to a min-heap where peek() and poll() always return the smallest element -- peek() is O(1) since the smallest is always at the root, while poll() is O(log n) since removing the root requires reorganizing the heap afterward. A max-heap requires an explicit reversing Comparator, like Collections.reverseOrder(). A classic use case is finding the kth largest element by maintaining a min-heap capped at size k, discarding the smallest whenever it grows past that -- the root ends up being exactly the kth largest survivor.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: PriorityQueue = a hospital ER queue, not a checkout line -- always treats (peek/poll) whoever's most urgent (smallest, by default), no matter arrival order. Want most-severe-first instead? Flip the ordering with a Comparator.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: Does PriorityQueue default to a min-heap or a max-heap?
How do you make a PriorityQueue behave as a max-heap?
Why is peek() faster than poll()?
How does capping a min-heap at size k help find the kth largest element?
